title: RA.965 Loidis
tracklist: Benjamin Brunn - I Prefer Elevators So Inagawa - Cabaret Smyglyssna - The Absence of Insects Fax - Braille Sieg Uber Die Sonne - Cleaning Windows (Ricardo Villalobos Remix) Rechenzentrum - Submarine Christoph Schindling - Half A Bottle Part III Neurotron - Portal Mikael Stavostrand - Stol Apoll - Unbehandelte Klangneurose SCSI-9 - Go To The Freeze Process - Contain Ernesto - Ennyah Makaton - Lebanon Hard Wired Microbox - Go Jira Dance Segeke - YrEn John Tejada, Arian Leviste - Modern Scene Savvas Ysatis - The Bombshell Dialogue - Role Models Mark Thibideau - Felt Steevio - Daze Hofuku Sochi - Sakunen Antonelli Electr. - Augustine Jonas Bering - Untitled Twotone - Re-Enactment Broker/ Dealer - Dig Deep Photo: Allison Tirrell

translation.content: In 2024, Brian Leeds prioritised pleasure. Under his revived Loidis alias, the Kansas-raised DJ and producer—AKA Huerco S.—released One Day, an alluring, emotionally-rich patchwork of dub techno and micro house. Enamoured by restrained, loopy sounds of '00s dance music, the album's eight tracks linger in the air, luxuriating in dubbed-out chords, swung beats and beefy bass vibrations. It's a sound he's coined, in typically teasing fashion, "dub mnml emo tech." All winks aside, it should be no wonder that One Day was the soothing balm we all needed. After an overload of hard and fast sounds, the softer (and Leeds might argue, more sincere) stylings of minimal and dub techno experienced a welcomed second wind. In the end, One Day ran out a comfortable winner for RA's Record of the Year. To celebrate, we decided to break our usual formula of no-repeats and invite Leeds back to the RA Podcast under a different name. "The prevailing trends in dance music are more and more maximalist," Leeds said. "I missed restraint, subtlety and sensuality." Clocking in at just under two hours, RA.965 is exactly that: from the echoes of Bar25 (keep an ear out for the Kompakt and Traum Schallplatten inclusions) to the tactile micro explorations of Steve Bailey and Hofuku Sochi. This entry to the RA Podcast is a portal into Leeds' idealised vision of the dance floor. It's a club mix, but don't let that confine you. However and wherever you choose to listen, RA.965 just asks you to do one thing: feel it out. – Rachel Grace Almeida As 2024 comes to a close, what are your reflections on this year? I've been lucky to play some amazing shows and festivals this year, especially in the US. Going into the new year, for me, it's important to help foster a better environment for weirdo music stateside. I've moved into a new place, met people who inspire me and finally got my studio set up after years of making tunes solely on headphones (RIP my ears). I've come to see how truly interconnected music can be. What was the idea behind this mix? It was recorded on an unseasonably warm November afternoon at my house in Philadelphia on some borrowed XDJs (shout out DJ Papaya). Around the start of the year, I'd begun compiling tunes that were influential to the Loidis project, for DJ use. Of those near-thousand songs, I narrowed it down to about 26. [The mix] is early '00s-focused; I don't think there's a tune on here that's more recent than 2008. Influences would be Villalobos and his loose approach to storytelling, John Tejada, Savvas Ysatis, Background Records, Perlon Records, Telegraph Records and generally spending way too much time on Beatport. In One Day, you've made RA's record of the year. Are you happy with the reception? In many ways, the Loidis project seems closer to what Huerco S. started out as. It's a return to more house/techno adjacent music. Perhaps I'm too aware of 20-year trend cycles, but here we are, two decades removed from the '00s, and the prevailing trends in dance music are more and more maximalist. I missed restraint, subtlety and sensuality. As much as I appreciate any acclaim for the record, I'd really prefer more bookings. So, if you're a promoter reading this: hit my line! You've called your Loidis output "dub mnml emo tech." What exactly does this mean? Just a lil' tongue-in-cheek, self-ascribed genre tag… funnier than saying third-wave minimal, dub techno, I suppose. Jokes aside, there are some defining characteristics: low-slung, emotive, moody, melodic dance music. What do you make of the current dub techno resurgence and how it's overlapping with micro house? How have the sounds evolved? I'm not too familiar with current music in this sphere. All that comes to mind is Shinichi Atobe (the GOAT), Teakup and the False Aralia label. You could easily make the case that artists in the West Mineral / Motion Ward / 3XL axis are inspired by dub techno, but I wouldn't say I know too many people making that kind of music in the pure sense. There's tons of potential, at least with micro and dub-related sound design. You could argue that some niche music in the plugg, digicore and rap world draw influence from the ambient, clicks'n'cuts or lowercase styles of the '00s, although perhaps indirectly. That said, I'd love to make a pop dub techno record. What's one thing you wish to take into 2025, and one thing you want to leave behind? In: love and sincerity. Out: all the bullshit.
translation.blurb: Huerco S. accompanies One Day, our favourite record of 2024, with two hours of minimal and dub introspections.
